name: Basque Country
history: The Basque Country has one of Europe's most distinct and ancient cultures, with evidence of human settlement dating back millennia. During the Middle Ages, the region developed its own fueros, or charters, granting it considerable autonomy within the Spanish kingdoms. The industrial revolution transformed the Basque territories, particularly around Bilbao, making them an economic powerhouse. The Spanish Civil War and Franco's dictatorship brought severe repression to Basque language and culture, though the region has since recovered its autonomy and celebrated heritage.
culturalNotes: The Basque people maintain a fiercely independent identity expressed through their unique language, distinctive cuisine, and strong regional pride. Traditional txoko social clubs, pelota ball games, and bertsolari poetry competitions remain central to Basque social life and cultural expression. The region is known for its culinary excellence, with pintxos bar snacks and innovative gastronomy drawing visitors worldwide.
bestTimeToVisit: Summer months offer the best weather for exploring the coast and cities.
languageNotes: Euskera, the Basque language, is a linguistic isolate unrelated to Spanish or any other European language, and has experienced a major revival since the end of Franco's regime. Common greetings include "Kaixo" (hello) and "Eskerrik asko" (thank you very much), and many locals appreciate visitors who make an effort to use Basque words.

places.name: Bilbao
places.description: The largest city in the Basque Country, transformed from an industrial port into a vibrant cultural hub anchored by the Guggenheim Museum. Its atmospheric old town, riverside promenades, and superb pintxo bars make it an essential stop.

activities.detail: Frank Gehry's titanium-clad masterpiece houses contemporary art and is itself the city's iconic landmark, complete with Jeff Koons' giant flower sculpture Puppy outside.
Explore the medieval Siete Calles (Seven Streets) packed with shops, churches, and pintxo bars around Plaza Nueva.
Take the century-old transporter bridge, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, across the Nervión estuary between Portugalete and Getxo.
Walk or cycle along the revitalized riverfront past modern bridges like Zubizuri and public art installations.

activities.text: Visit the Museo de Bellas Artes
activities.detail: See an outstanding collection of Basque, Spanish, and European art spanning from medieval times to the 20th century.
Ride the funicular up Mount Artxanda for panoramic views over the city and surrounding green hills.
Browse one of Europe's largest covered markets, a great spot for fresh produce, seafood, and casual pintxos.

food.detail: Hop between bars in Casco Viejo and Plaza Nueva sampling bite-sized creations like gilda (anchovy, olive, pepper) and txalupa (stuffed pastry boats).
Salt cod slow-cooked in olive oil and garlic, emulsified into a signature Basque sauce.
Thick-cut, bone-in beef chop grilled over coals, a beloved staple of Basque cuisine served rare and smoky.
A hearty tuna and potato stew traditionally made by Basque fishermen, warming and rustic.
Pair the slightly sparkling, crisp local white wine with fresh anchovies or grilled fish.
Finish a meal with creamy arroz con leche or a wedge of smoky sheep's milk Idiazabal cheese, often with quince paste.

culture.detail: Pintxos are typically eaten standing at the bar with a small drink, and you keep track of your toothpicks to tally the bill.
Euskara (Basque) is co-official with Spanish, so many signs and menus appear in both languages.
Many shops close for a few hours in early afternoon, and locals eat dinner quite late, often after 9pm.
Basque culture and identity are strong and distinct from the rest of Spain, so showing interest in local customs and language is appreciated.

places.name: San Sebastián
places.description: An elegant seaside city famed for its crescent-shaped La Concha beach and one of the highest concentrations of Michelin-starred restaurants in the world. Its old quarter is packed with legendary pintxo bars, making it a food lover's paradise.

activities.detail: Stroll the curving promenade along one of Europe's most beautiful urban beaches, framed by Monte Igueldo and Monte Urgull.
Climb the wooded hill above the old town to the Castillo de la Mota and the Sagrado Corazón statue for panoramic bay views.
Take the historic funicular up Monte Igueldo for sweeping views and an old-fashioned amusement park at the summit.
Wander the narrow lanes packed with pintxo bars, tiny plazas, and the Baroque Basilica de Santa María del Coro.
Discover Basque history, art, and ethnography in a renovated 16th-century convent at the foot of Monte Urgull.

activities.text: See the Peine del Viento sculptures
activities.detail: Admire Chillida's iconic iron sculptures embedded in the rocks at the western end of Ondarreta beach.
Visit this nearby fishing village, birthplace of Juan Sebastián Elcano, known for grilled fish and Getariako Txakolina wine.

food.detail: Hop between bars in the old town sampling bite-sized creations like gilda (anchovy, olive, guindilla pepper) and tortilla de patata.
Try this classic Basque salt cod dish emulsified in olive oil and garlic into a silky, garlicky sauce.
Seek out a thick, well-aged rib steak cooked over coals, a Basque grill-house specialty.
Sample this smoky sheep's milk cheese from the Basque highlands, often paired with quince paste.
Drink the slightly sparkling, crisp local white wine, traditionally poured from a height to aerate it.
Splurge on a multi-course meal at one of the city's renowned fine-dining restaurants showcasing modern Basque cuisine.

culture.detail: Order drinks and pintxos at the bar, keep your toothpicks and napkins as they're often used to tally your bill.
Euskara (Basque) is widely spoken and seen on signs alongside Spanish, reflecting strong regional identity.
Locals typically eat dinner from 9pm onward, so restaurants and bars stay lively late into the evening.
July and August bring large crowds to La Concha, so arrive early to claim a good spot on the sand.

places.name: Vitoria-Gasteiz
places.description: The Basque Country's official capital, known for its beautifully preserved medieval old town shaped like an almond and its green urban design. It offers a quieter, more authentic Basque experience than the coastal cities with excellent cathedrals and squares.

activities.detail: Explore this Gothic cathedral's ongoing restoration via the unique 'Open for Restoration' guided tour that lets visitors see archaeological work in progress.
Relax in this lively main square lined with cafes, marking the boundary between the medieval old town and the 19th-century expansion.
Wander the almond-shaped old town's narrow, concentric streets that trace the city's medieval origins on a hilltop.
Visit this restored Renaissance palace overlooking the square, now used as a cultural center with rotating exhibitions.
See a strong collection of contemporary Basque and Spanish art in this modern museum near the city center.
Cycle or stroll the ring of parks and wetlands encircling the city, a pioneering example of Basque urban ecological design.
Tour the city's 'new' Neo-Gothic cathedral, notable for its towering spires and stained glass.
Photograph this elegant arched passageway connecting the upper old town to the lower expansion, a clever piece of 18th-century urban engineering.

food.detail: Hop between bars on this atmospheric medieval street, known locally as Kutxilleria, for creative Basque pintxos and local wine.
Try this hearty stew of local white beans, often cooked with chorizo and pork, a signature Alavese comfort dish.
Seek out these prized wild spring mushrooms, a seasonal delicacy served simply sautéed with garlic and egg.
Pair your meal with this slightly sparkling, crisp white wine typical of the Basque Country.
Sample this classic Basque salt-cod dish emulsified in olive oil and garlic, found in many traditional restaurants around town.

culture.detail: Street names and signs appear in both Basque (Euskera) and Spanish, reflecting the region's dual linguistic identity.
If visiting in early August, expect the city's biggest festival, marked by the symbolic descent of Celedón and days of street parties.
Vitoria-Gasteiz offers a calmer, more local rhythm of life compared to San Sebastián or Bilbao, with fewer tourist crowds.
Many small shops close for a few hours in early afternoon, so plan errands and museum visits accordingly.

places.name: Guernica
places.description: A historic town famed as the spiritual home of Basque culture and tragically as the site of the 1937 bombing immortalized in Picasso's painting. Visitors come to see the Peace Museum, the historic assembly house, and the ancient oak tree symbolizing Basque liberties.

activities.text: Casa de Juntas and the Gernika Oak
activities.detail: Visit the Basque parliament building and the sacred oak tree outside it that symbolizes centuries of Basque self-governance.

activities.text: Museo de la Paz de Gernika
activities.detail: This Peace Museum documents the 1937 bombing of the town and explores themes of reconciliation and human rights.
Explore the history, geography, and culture of the wider Basque Country through interactive exhibits.

activities.text: Parque de los Pueblos de Europa
activities.detail: Stroll through this park featuring sculptures by Henry Moore and Eduardo Chillida dedicated to peace.

activities.text: See the tile mural of Picasso's Guernica
activities.detail: A large ceramic reproduction of Picasso's famous painting stands as a public memorial near the town center.

activities.text: Monday Market at Plaza de los Fueros
activities.detail: Join locals at this centuries-old open-air market for fresh produce, cheeses, and regional crafts.

activities.text: Day trip to the Urdaibai Biosphere Reserve
activities.detail: Explore the nearby estuary and marshlands, a UNESCO-recognized haven for birdwatching and hiking.

food.detail: Small green peppers from the region, lightly fried in olive oil and sprinkled with coarse salt.
A hearty Basque fisherman's stew made with tuna, potatoes, and peppers.
A slightly sparkling, dry white wine from the Basque coast, traditionally poured from a height to aerate it.
A smoky sheep's milk cheese often served with quince paste or walnuts.

food.text: Pintxos crawl in the old town
food.detail: Sample small savory bites at local bars, a beloved Basque tradition of casual, social eating.
Thick-cut beef chops grilled over wood coals, a classic Basque delicacy.

culture.detail: The town's famous market has been held every Monday for centuries, filling the streets with vendors and locals.
Locals deeply value their language, and greetings like 'Kaixo' or 'Eskerrik asko' are appreciated.
The 1937 bombing remains a solemn part of local memory, so visit memorial sites with sensitivity.
Many shops close for a few hours in early afternoon, following the traditional Spanish siesta schedule.

places.name: Hondarribia
places.description: A charming fishing town on the French border known for its colorful balconied houses and well-preserved fortified old quarter. Its picturesque marina and proximity to the Bidasoa river make it a delightful coastal stop.

activities.detail: Stroll the cobbled old quarter with its coats of arms, stone walls, and centuries-old fortified houses.

activities.text: See the colorful balconies of Calle Mayor and Calle San Pedro
activities.detail: Admire the wooden balconies painted in deep reds and greens, a signature feature of Basque fishing town architecture.

activities.text: Visit the Castle of Charles V
activities.detail: This 10th-century fortress turned Parador hotel dominates the old town and once defended the border with France.

activities.text: Explore the marina and fishing port
activities.detail: Watch working fishing boats unload their catch in a lively harbor still central to local life.

activities.text: Walk or cycle to Cabo Higuer lighthouse
activities.detail: A scenic coastal path leads to this lighthouse with sweeping views over the Bay of Biscay and French coast.

activities.text: Take the small ferry across the Bidasoa to Hendaye
activities.detail: A quick boat crossing lets you hop into France for lunch or a look at another side of the border region.
A sheltered sandy beach near the marina, popular with families for calm swimming waters.

activities.text: Visit the Ermita de Nuestra Señora de Guadalupe
activities.detail: This hilltop hermitage offers panoramic views and is the focus of a traditional pilgrimage each September.

food.detail: Spider crab meat mixed with tomato and brandy, often served baked in its own shell.

food.text: Pintxos crawl in the old town
food.detail: Hop between small bars along Calle San Pedro for bite-sized Basque tapas topped with seafood or cured meats.

food.text: Bacalao al pil-pil or a la vizcaína
food.detail: Salted cod cooked in olive oil emulsion or a rich red pepper sauce, a Basque coastal staple.
A warm corn flatbread folded around grilled Basque chorizo sausage, a popular street snack.
A slightly sparkling, crisp white wine from the Basque coast, traditionally poured from a height to aerate it.
Grilled or marinated straight from the local fishing fleet, best enjoyed at a portside restaurant.

culture.detail: Each September 8th the town holds a historic military procession commemorating a 1638 siege victory, drawing huge crowds.

culture.text: Basque and Spanish are both used
culture.detail: Street signs and menus often appear in Euskara as well as Spanish, reflecting strong local Basque identity.
Lunch is typically served from 1:30-3:30pm and dinner rarely starts before 8:30pm, as is standard in Spain.

culture.text: The border crossing to France is casual and quick
culture.detail: Locals frequently cross via bridge or ferry to Hendaye for shopping or dining, so bring ID just in case.

places.name: Getaria
places.description: A picturesque fishing village famous as the birthplace of designer Cristóbal Balenciaga and for its grilled fish restaurants along the harbor. Its distinctive mouse-shaped headland and txakoli vineyards make it a scenic coastal highlight.

activities.detail: Housed in a restored palace, this museum showcases the work of the legendary couturier who was born in Getaria in 1895.

activities.text: Walk out to El Ratón de Getaria
activities.detail: Stroll along the breakwater to the mouse-shaped headland that shelters the harbor and offers sweeping coastal views.

activities.text: Explore the Church of San Salvador
activities.detail: This 14th-century Gothic church has a sloping floor and is built so that its passageway once served as a public street.
Visit one of the hillside wineries above town to learn how the crisp, slightly sparkling Getariako Txakolina is produced and poured from height.
Watch boats unload their catch and see the statue honoring Juan Sebastián Elcano, the Getaria-born navigator who completed the first circumnavigation of the globe.
This small sheltered beach beside the harbor is a pleasant spot to swim or sunbathe after exploring the old town.
Hike up the headland for panoramic views over the village, harbor, and surrounding txakoli-covered hillsides.

food.detail: Getaria's asadores are famous for cooking whole turbot over open charcoal grills along the harbor front.
A crisp, slightly fizzy local white wine traditionally poured from a height to aerate it before drinking.
The town has a long history of anchovy fishing and salting, and cured anchoas are a prized local specialty.
Alongside turbot, other fresh Cantabrian catches are simply grilled with olive oil and sea salt at portside restaurants.
Look for local Idiazabal cheese paired with txakoli as a simple, authentic Basque tasting combination.

culture.detail: The grilled fish asadores are popular with both tourists and locals, so booking a table in advance is wise, especially on weekends.
Getaria remains an active fishing port, so mornings are busy with boats and nets that visitors should be mindful of along the quay.
As with many Basque villages, modest attire is appreciated when entering the Church of San Salvador.

culture.text: Learn a little Basque and Spanish
culture.detail: Both Euskara and Spanish are spoken locally, and a simple 'eskerrik asko' (thank you) is warmly received.

places.name: Zumaia
places.description: A coastal town renowned for the dramatic Flysch rock formations, layered cliffs revealing 60 million years of geological history. It's a favorite for scenic coastal walks and was a filming location for Game of Thrones.

activities.detail: Walk along Itzurun Beach to see the dramatic layered flysch rock formations that record 60 million years of geological history.
Visit this small geology center to understand the formation of the flysch and the Basque Coast Geopark before heading to the cliffs.

activities.text: Boat trip along the Flysch coast
activities.detail: Take a boat excursion from Zumaia or nearby Getaria to view the towering flysch cliffs from the sea for a full perspective.
Climb to this small chapel perched on a headland overlooking the sea, a scenic spot also used in filming locations.
Recognize Itzurun Beach as the filming location for Dragonstone scenes in Game of Thrones Season 7.
Hike a section of the Basque Coast trail offering sweeping views of cliffs, sea, and countryside between Zumaia and Deba.
Wander the historic center with its narrow streets, the Church of San Pedro, and views over the Urola River estuary.
Visit the former home and studio of the famous Basque painter, located just outside town, showcasing his works and art collection.

food.detail: A hearty Basque tuna and potato stew traditionally made by fishermen, perfect for the coastal climate.
Try this slightly sparkling, crisp local white wine, often poured from a height to aerate it before serving with pintxos.
Salted cod cooked slowly in olive oil and garlic to create a naturally emulsified sauce, a Basque culinary classic.
Squid cooked in their own ink, served as a rich, dark sauce often accompanied by rice.
Sample small Basque tapas at local bars, ranging from anchovies to txistorra sausage skewers.
Finish a meal with this traditional Basque layered dessert of cream, sponge cake, and caramel.

culture.text: Check tide times before visiting the flysch
culture.detail: The best views and safest walking along Itzurun Beach depend on low tide, so plan your visit accordingly.
Expect signs in Euskara (Basque) alongside Spanish, reflecting the strong regional identity of the area.
Many small shops and restaurants close for a few hours in the early afternoon, typical of Basque coastal towns.
Since Itzurun Beach draws Game of Thrones fans, be respectful of other visitors and any restricted areas near the cliffs.

places.name: Lekeitio
places.description: A traditional fishing town with a beautiful gothic church and a stunning beach split by a small tidal island. Its unspoiled charm and lively harbor make it one of the most attractive stops along the Basque coast.

activities.text: Walk to Isla de San Nicolás
activities.detail: At low tide a sandy causeway emerges letting you stroll out to this small tidal island right off the main beach.

activities.text: Visit Santa María la Real Church
activities.detail: This striking Gothic church with a Renaissance tower dominates the old town and is one of the finest examples of Basque coastal religious architecture.
Watch colorful fishing boats come and go and see the daily catch unloaded at this still-active working port.
Follow coastal trails east of town to this dramatic limestone headland with sweeping Bay of Biscay views.
Rent a kayak to explore the sheltered bay, sea caves, and the tidal island from the water.

activities.text: Stroll the old quarter's arcaded streets
activities.detail: Wander narrow lanes lined with balconied townhouses and shaded arcades near the town hall square.
A long sandy beach ideal for swimming and surfing lessons, less crowded than the main town beach.

activities.text: Take a boat trip along the coast
activities.detail: Join a short scenic cruise from the harbor to admire Lekeitio's cliffs and hidden coves from the sea.

food.detail: A hearty tuna and potato stew traditionally made by Basque fishermen, often found on menus here in summer months.
Pair the local slightly sparkling white wine with simply prepared boquerones or anchoas straight off the boats.
Delicate hake or cod throats cooked in garlic and olive oil, a prized Basque delicacy served at portside restaurants.

food.text: Pintxos crawl in the old town
food.detail: Hop between small bars near the harbor for bite-sized pintxos topped with seafood, cheese, or cured meats.

food.text: Grilled fresh fish at the port restaurants
food.detail: Sample whatever the boats brought in that morning, often cooked simply a la brasa with olive oil and garlic.

culture.text: Check the tide tables before visiting the island
culture.detail: The causeway to Isla de San Nicolás is only walkable at low tide, so plan your visit around local tide schedules.

culture.text: Respect the Antzar Eguna tradition debate
culture.detail: Lekeitio's September goose festival is historic but controversial for animal welfare reasons, so be aware of the sensitivities if visiting during festival dates.
Euskara is commonly used alongside Spanish, and locals appreciate visitors attempting simple greetings like 'kaixo' or 'eskerrik asko'.
Restaurants typically serve lunch after 1:30pm and dinner from 8:30pm or later, in keeping with Spanish dining hours.

places.name: Mundaka
places.description: A small fishing village world-renowned among surfers for having one of the best left-hand waves in Europe. It sits at the mouth of the Urdaibai estuary, a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve rich in wildlife.

activities.detail: Watch or paddle out at this world-famous left-hand barrel that breaks over a sandbar at the estuary mouth, best from autumn to early spring with a strong swell and outgoing tide.

activities.text: Climb to the Santa Catalina lookout
activities.detail: A short walk above the harbor leads to a small chapel and viewpoint with sweeping views over the wave, the breakwater and the Bay of Biscay.
Stroll among the moored fishing boats and stone quaysides of Mundaka's compact harbor, still active with local txipiroi and anchovy boats.

activities.text: Cross the estuary to Laida Beach
activities.detail: Take the seasonal foot-passenger boat or drive around to Laida, a wide sandy beach on the far side of the Urdaibai estuary popular for swimming and sunset views.
The tidal marshes and reedbeds of this UNESCO Biosphere Reserve host herons, spoonbills and migratory waders, especially visible from hides near Gautegiz Arteaga.
Guided tours (limited daily entries, book ahead) show a short section of Paleolithic cave paintings inside this Urdaibai landmark just inland from Mundaka.
See the symbolic Tree of Gernika, the Peace Museum and the lively Monday farmers' market in this historic Basque town a short drive away.
Rent a kayak in Mundaka to explore the calm inner waters of the Ría de Mundaka at low tide, weaving past sandbanks and marshland.

food.detail: Salt cod slow-cooked in olive oil and garlic until the sauce emulsifies into a silky pil-pil, a Basque coastal classic found in Mundaka's small restaurants.
A hearty Basque fisherman's stew of bonito tuna, potatoes and peppers, traditionally made aboard boats and still served in local taverns.
Pair the crisp, slightly sparkling local white wine, Bizkaiko Txakolina, with anchovies or sardines landed just up the coast in Bermeo.
Baby squid stewed in their own ink, a rich dark sauce classically mopped up with bread in Basque coastal villages.
Graze on small counter snacks like stuffed peppers, cod croquettes or grilled txistorra at the handful of bars overlooking Mundaka's port.
Head to nearby Bermeo's fish auction and market to see the daily catch that supplies Mundaka's kitchens, from hake to percebes (goose barnacles).

culture.detail: Mundaka's wave draws a dedicated local crew, so newer surfers should watch etiquette closely and avoid crowding experienced riders at peak swell.
Boat crossings to Laida, the quality of the surf break and even walkable paths along the estuary all shift with the tide, so check schedules before planning.
Euskara is widely spoken and seen on signage alongside Spanish, and a simple 'eskerrik asko' (thank you) is appreciated.

culture.text: Village pace slows outside surf season
culture.detail: Mundaka is small and quiet, with shops and restaurants keeping limited hours in winter outside the main summer and autumn surf months.

places.name: Urdaibai Biosphere Reserve
places.description: A UNESCO-designated estuary and wetland teeming with birdlife, marshes, and forested hills between Gernika and Mundaka. It offers excellent hiking, birdwatching, and access to unspoiled beaches and cliffs.

activities.detail: Observe herons, spoonbills, and migratory waders from elevated hides overlooking the marshes near Gautegiz Arteaga.
Watch or ride the legendary left-hand river-mouth wave, one of the best in Europe for experienced surfers.
Visit the Peace Museum, the Casa de Juntas, and the famous oak tree symbolizing Basque self-governance.
Tour a UNESCO-listed cave near Kortezubi with Paleolithic paintings and engravings dating back 14,000 years.
Stroll a wide sandy beach at the estuary mouth with views across to Mundaka and the wooded hills.

activities.text: Boat trip on the Urdaibai estuary
activities.detail: Take a small guided boat ride through the tidal marshes to spot herons, cormorants, and shifting sandbanks.

activities.text: Hike to San Juan de Gaztelugatxe
activities.detail: Climb the dramatic stone stairway to the islet chapel perched on a rocky outcrop just west of the reserve.
Follow coastal paths above sheer cliffs between Elantxobe and Ibarrangelu for sweeping Bay of Biscay views.

food.detail: A hearty Basque tuna and potato stew traditionally made by fishermen along this coast.
Try the crisp, slightly sparkling local white wine, often poured from height in nearby bodegas and pintxo bars.
Salt cod slow-cooked in olive oil and garlic until it forms a natural emulsified sauce, a Basque classic.
Sample small bites like anchovies, tortilla, and cheese at bars around Gernika, especially lively on market Mondays.
A grilled corn-flour flatbread folded around spicy txistorra sausage, a popular festival snack.
Look for grilled sardines, elvers, or spider crab served at restaurants overlooking the Mundaka or Bermeo harbor.

culture.detail: Every Monday the town hosts a large traditional market with local produce, cheese, and crafts, a centuries-old tradition.
Euskera is widely used alongside Spanish on signs and menus, reflecting strong regional identity.
The wave break has a strong local surf culture, so newcomers should observe lineup etiquette carefully.

culture.text: Check tide times for estuary visits
culture.detail: Much of Urdaibai's beauty depends on tides, so plan beach or boat visits around local tide charts.

places.name: Gorbeia Natural Park
places.description: The largest protected natural area in the Basque Country, centered on Mount Gorbeia, offering extensive hiking trails through beech forests and pastures. It's a favorite escape for nature lovers and mountaineers seeking dramatic highland scenery.

activities.text: Summit Gorbeia via the Iron Cross
activities.detail: Hike to the 1,482m summit marked by the iconic Gurutze Aundia cross, the classic goal for most visitors to the park.
Wander the karstic plateau of Peña de Itxina, riddled with sinkholes, caves and prehistoric dolmens.

activities.text: Visit the Sanctuary of Nuestra Señora de Oro
activities.detail: This mountain sanctuary at the foot of Gorbeia is a traditional starting point for pilgrims and hikers alike.
After heavy rain this seasonal cascade near the park's edge becomes one of the tallest waterfalls in the Basque Country.
A well-preserved Neolithic burial dolmen sits along a highland trail, reflecting the area's ancient pastoral history.
This mountain refuge makes a good base for multi-day treks or an early assault on the summit.

activities.text: Walk through the Pagomakurre beech forest
activities.detail: Follow shaded paths through ancient beechwoods that turn fiery orange in autumn.

activities.text: Spot native Pottoka ponies and Latxa sheep
activities.detail: Semi-wild Basque ponies and sheep graze freely across the park's highland pastures.

food.detail: Sample this smoky, raw sheep's milk cheese produced by shepherds who graze their flocks on Gorbeia's slopes.
A warm corn-flour flatbread folded around grilled spicy sausage, a favorite quick bite at rural fairs and refuges.
A hearty bean stew often served in nearby villages like Zeanuri and Areatza after a day of hiking.
Dry, tangy cider poured from height is a traditional accompaniment to grilled meats in local cider houses.
Thick-cut grilled beef chops from old dairy cows, a prized regional specialty found in area caserío restaurants.
Sweet layered pastries typical of Basque bakeries make a satisfying end to a mountain meal in nearby towns.

culture.detail: Mist and rain can roll in suddenly on Gorbeia, so pack layers and waterproofs even in summer.
Ponies, cattle and sheep roam freely across the park, so close gates and give animals space on trails.

culture.text: Follow the GR and PR trail markings
culture.detail: Well-marked long-distance and local paths crisscross the park, but a map or GPS is wise since signage can be sparse in fog.
Village signs and trail markers are often in Euskara, so learning a few basic Basque greetings is appreciated.

places.name: San Juan de Gaztelugatxe
places.description: A tiny islet crowned by a hermitage, connected to the mainland by a dramatic zigzagging stone bridge, made globally famous as a filming location for Game of Thrones. Climbing its 200-plus steps rewards visitors with stunning Cantabrian Sea views.

activities.text: Climb the stone stairway to the hermitage
activities.detail: Ascend the famous zigzagging bridge and the more than 200 stone steps up to the small hermitage of San Juan Bautista perched on the islet's summit.

activities.text: Ring the chapel bell three times
activities.detail: Follow the old local custom of ringing the bell inside the hermitage three times and making a wish, said to ward off bad luck.

activities.text: Photograph the zigzag bridge from the viewpoint
activities.detail: Head to the Mirador de San Juan de Gaztelugatxe on the approach path for the classic postcard view of the winding stone bridge snaking out to the islet.

activities.text: Walk the coastal path from Bakio
activities.detail: Hike the scenic cliff-top trail connecting Bakio to Gaztelugatxe for dramatic Cantabrian coastline views before reaching the islet.

activities.text: Spot Game of Thrones filming locations
activities.detail: Recognize the islet and surrounding coastline as Dragonstone from Game of Thrones, a pilgrimage spot for fans of the series.

activities.text: Visit nearby San Pelaio and the small port
activities.detail: Explore the quiet hamlet of San Pelaio just above the site, with a small harbor and additional coastal viewpoints.

food.detail: Sample this classic Basque salt-cod dish emulsified in olive oil and garlic, found in restaurants along the nearby coast.
Try the crisp, slightly sparkling local white wine typical of the Bizkaia coast, often poured with a dramatic high splash.
Enjoy anchovies fresh from the Cantabrian Sea, simply grilled or marinated in vinegar as boquerones.
Warm up with this hearty Basque fisherman's stew of tuna, potatoes, and peppers, popular in coastal villages nearby.
Head to the fishing town of Bermeo a short drive away for a pintxo crawl featuring seafood-topped bites and local cider.

culture.detail: The site and its limited parking get extremely busy midday, especially in summer, so early morning visits are recommended.
Access to the islet requires a reserved time slot booked in advance, especially during peak tourist season.
The steep stone steps and uneven bridge surface can be slippery, so proper footwear is advised over sandals.
The chapel is still an active place of worship, so visitors should behave respectfully and keep noise to a minimum inside.

places.name: Oñati
places.description: A historic town in the Basque interior known for its striking Renaissance university building and as the gateway to the sacred Arantzazu sanctuary and Aizkorri mountains. It offers a glimpse of traditional Basque architecture away from the coast.

activities.detail: Wander the arcaded Renaissance courtyard of this 16th-century university building, the first in the Basque Country and one of Spain's finest civil Plateresque monuments.
Admire the baroque facade of the Ayuntamiento with its balconies and coats of arms, then linger at a terrace on this arcaded main square.
Visit this striking modernist mountain sanctuary with a spiky facade by Sáenz de Oiza, doors by Chillida, and frescoes by Oteiza, perched dramatically above a gorge.
Set off from Arantzazu toward Aizkorri, the highest peak in Gipuzkoa, through beech forests and limestone karst scenery sacred to Basque shepherding culture.
Cross the small stone bridges over the Deba's tributary streams while strolling Kale Zaharra and Bidebarrieta, lined with noble houses and coats of arms.

activities.text: San Miguel Church and hanging bridge
activities.detail: See the church built partly over the river, with a curious enclosed bridge-passage crossing the water beside it.
If visiting in season, look for traces of Oñati's unique carnival characters like the Zaldiko and Ziripot, among the most distinctive in the Basque Country.

activities.text: Day trip to Idiazabal cheese country
activities.detail: Explore the surrounding hills where shepherds still produce smoked Idiazabal cheese, a protected Basque specialty.

food.detail: Sample this smoky sheep's-milk cheese from local flocks, often served with walnuts and quince paste.
Try the town's hearty red bean stew, traditionally cooked with chorizo, pork, and cabbage.
Eat a warm corn-flour flatbread filled with grilled txistorra sausage, a favorite snack at local festivals.
Visit a nearby sagardotegi to drink natural cider poured from height, paired with cod omelette and grilled steak.
Finish a meal with this layered Basque sponge, cream, and caramel dessert popular in Gipuzkoa's inland towns.

culture.detail: Euskera is widely spoken day-to-day, so greetings like 'Kaixo' and 'Eskerrik asko' are appreciated.
Many small shops and some restaurants close on Sunday afternoons and Mondays, especially outside peak season.
The sanctuary is an active place of worship, so dress modestly and keep voices low inside the basilica.
Carnival and the Corpus Christi 'Corpus' processions with flower carpets are major local events worth planning a visit around.

places.name: Laguardia
places.description: A fortified medieval hilltop town in Basque wine country (Rioja Alavesa), surrounded by vineyards and known for wineries built into its ancient tunnels. Its walled old town and panoramic vineyard views make it a top day trip from Vitoria-Gasteiz.

activities.detail: Stroll the well-preserved ramparts and stone gates that still encircle the old town, offering sweeping views over the vineyards of Rioja Alavesa.

activities.text: Iglesia de Santa María de los Reyes
activities.detail: Marvel at the extraordinary 14th-century polychrome Gothic portico, one of the best-preserved painted church facades in Spain.
Tour the centuries-old cuevas carved beneath the town, many still used as working bodegas for aging Rioja wine.
An interactive museum near the old town explaining Rioja Alavesa winemaking, with tastings and a short multimedia film.
Admire Santiago Calatrava's undulating aluminum-and-wood winery set dramatically against the Sierra de Cantabria, just outside town.

activities.text: Climb San Juan Bautista bell tower
activities.detail: Climb this church tower for one of the best panoramic views of the walled town and surrounding vineyard patchwork.
Look for the carved coats of arms above house doorways along the main street, remnants of noble medieval families.

activities.text: Day trip to a hillside vineyard viewpoint
activities.detail: Just outside Laguardia, pull over at a roadside mirador for classic postcard views of vines rolling toward the mountains.

food.detail: Lamb chops grilled over burning vine cuttings, a signature Rioja Alavesa preparation often served at wine harvest gatherings.
A hearty potato and chorizo stew simmered with local paprika, a classic regional comfort dish.
Sample robust reds directly at a bodega built into the town's ancient tunnels, often paired with a cellar tour.
Snails cooked in a spicy tomato and chorizo sauce, a traditional tapa found in local taverns.
Hop between small bars for bite-sized pintxos topped with local cheese, cured meats, or piquillo peppers.
A firm sheep's milk cheese from the region, often served as a simple starter alongside a glass of crianza.

culture.detail: Cars are largely restricted inside the compact walled town, so use the designated lots just beyond the old gates.
Many underground wine cellars and wineries require reservations, especially for tours in English or during harvest season.

culture.text: Visit during the grape harvest festival
culture.detail: Late September's Fiestas de la Virgen de los Remedios and the wine harvest celebrations bring processions, tastings, and traditional dance.
Many shops and small bodegas close for a couple of hours around lunchtime, following typical Spanish siesta hours.

restaurants.name: Akelarre
restaurants.description: Three-Michelin-star restaurant with innovative takes on Basque tradition and breathtaking Bay of Biscay views.
restaurants.mustOrder: Kokoतsu (Sea urchin with kelp and uni)

restaurants.name: Martín Berasategui
restaurants.description: Three-Michelin-star temple of haute cuisine near San Sebastián with exquisite tasting menus and impeccable service.

restaurants.name: Arzak
restaurants.address: Avenida Alcalde José Elosegui, 273, San Sebastián
restaurants.description: Three-Michelin-star iconic restaurant where creativity meets tradition in dishes that challenge and delight.
restaurants.mustOrder: Txuleta de bonito (Bonito chop with Basque technique)

restaurants.name: Asador Etxebarri
restaurants.description: One-Michelin-star temple to grilled meats and fish cooked over open flames in a traditional txoko setting.

restaurants.name: Víctor Montes
restaurants.description: Legendary cooking club atmosphere with excellent pintxos and hearty Basque fare in historic Plaza Nueva.

restaurants.name: Guria
restaurants.description: Family-run classic in the old town serving generous portions of authentic Basque cuisine since 1945.
restaurants.mustOrder: Kokotxas de bacalao (Cod cheeks in pil-pil sauce)

restaurants.name: El Perro Chico
restaurants.description: Cozy pintxos bar and wine shop where locals gather for creative small bites and exceptional Basque wines.

restaurants.name: Tolona
restaurants.description: Modern Basque kitchen in Vitoria showcasing seasonal ingredients with refined technique and elegant plating.
restaurants.mustOrder: Piquillo peppers with goat cheese and jamón

restaurants.name: Las Cubanas
restaurants.description: Lively tapas bar beloved by locals for creative pintxos, croquetas, and a vibrant atmosphere in central Vitoria.

restaurants.name: Elkano
restaurants.description: Beachfront institution in Getaria famous for its txuleta de cherne (grilled fish) and stunning coastal views.

restaurants.name: Andra Mari
restaurants.description: One-Michelin-star seafood specialist with daily catches prepared simply to highlight pristine flavors.
restaurants.mustOrder: Grilled turbot with local Txakoli wine

restaurants.name: La Caja Mágica
restaurants.description: Intimate restaurant in medieval Hondarribia serving creative, elegant dishes in a charming 16th-century setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: Langostino a la sal con aguacate

restaurants.name: Sebastián
restaurants.description: Casual neighborhood gem in the historic quarter offering authentic home-style Basque cooking and warm hospitality.

restaurants.name: Alameda
restaurants.address: Calle Ramón y Cajal, 2, San Sebastián
restaurants.description: One-Michelin-star restaurant in modern Gros neighborhood focused on seasonal products and precise technique.
restaurants.mustOrder: Hake with cider sauce and apple

restaurants.name: Casa Montaña
restaurants.description: Historic pintxos bar and institution since 1957 where locals queue for exquisite small bites and vermouth.
restaurants.mustOrder: Jamón ibérico pintxo with manchego cheese
